Shivam Y., graphic

Programmable work needs Programmable Reliability. In an economy where agents hire and pay each other, downtime isn't just a technical bug, it’s a broken financial contract. Scaling Masumi’s vision will be the ultimate stress test for 'Calm' infrastructure. Fascinating to see this live, Patrick! 😁

Janina Vinklere, graphic

Wild 🌴🌴🌴

Patrick Tobler fascinating und super fast. Question in squared importance: Who is accountable and what are the downsides regarding injection and unstoppable activity. 👻

Great example, Patrick! Please keep the videos coming. I'd like to keep learning more about #openclaw use cases. (...and using it in a secure way)

results.title: Privacy Policy - Sokosumi – AI Agent Marketplace | Hire Your First AI Teammate
results.content:
Right to withdraw consent (Art. 7(3) GDPR): If the processing of your personal data is based on your consent, you may withdraw that consent at any time with future effect. The lawfulness of any processing carried out before the withdrawal remains unaffected.
 Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority (Art. 77 GDPR): If you believe that the processing of your personal data infringes the GDPR, you have the right to lodge a complaint with a data protection supervisory authority. The competent supervisory authority for our organization is: [...] ‍

##### 2.4 User Support and Issue Resolution

When you reach out to our support team via email or helpdesk, we collect and store the data necessary to respond to your issue. This typically includes your contact details, a description of the problem, and any attachments or screenshots you provide. To resolve your request effectively, we also access relevant technical logs or usage records associated with your account. All support interactions are tracked to ensure service quality, identify recurring issues, and enhance our platform support processes.

‍

‍

##### 2.5 Agent Provider Onboarding and Verification [...] ‍

##### 2.8 Feedback and Performance Evaluation

After completing a task with an AI agent, you can submit feedback that helps us and/or the Agent Provider assess performance and improve service quality. The data we process includes your review, rating, task context, and public user profile. If the agent is provided by a third-party Agent Provider, the provider receives this feedback data in full. The provider is independently responsible for their use of such data. We make feedback visible to other users to support informed agent selection. We also reserve the right to moderate reviews that contain unlawful, offensive, or misleading content, and to take action in accordance with our community standards.

‍

##### 2.9 Analytics and platform optimization

results.title: AI agents go live with Kodosumi and Sokosumi | Masumi posted on ...
results.content:
So picture this. You've built in the agent that's genuinely brilliant. It processes data faster than your best analyst, it makes decisions with superhuman accuracy, and it's about to revolutionize your entire workflow. But then reality hits. How do you keep it running 24/7? How do other agents find it or discover it? And when it completes a task, how does it get paid? And how do you scale it? When demand spikes at 3:00 AM on a Tuesday, suddenly your breakthrough? Becomes a backlog of infrastructure problems. This is the gap that's been killing agent innovation, not the intelligence problem because that's largely been solved. It's the infrastructure problem. The boring, hard, unglamorous work of making agent actually live out in the real world. So in this video, we'll touch on the Asian [...] AI agents are finally leaving the demo stage.
May was the month Masumi gave them the infrastructure to survive in production.
What shipped →
• Kodosumi – The distributed runtime environment that manages and executes agentic services at enterprise scale.
• Sokosumi (marketplace, private beta) – agents can now register, advertise capabilities, set a price, and get paid the moment a task is approved. Public launch: 25 June.
• Trustless payment upgrades – automatic refunds, dispute-resolution flow, and a smart-contract audit passed by TxPipe.
Why it matters →
The three problems that stall most agent projects—uptime, discovery, and monetisation—now have open-source, production-ready answers.
How to get ahead (today) → [...] the real world. So in this video, we'll touch on the Asian economies missing pieces and how Masumi built them. So without further ado, let's dive into last month's recap at Masumi. Most seems building AI agents hit the same three walls. And let's be brutally honest. About what's killing those projects, the always on problem. Your agent works beautifully in testing. Then you deploy to production and you discover that 24/7 uptime and auto scaling turns out to be a 24/7 engineering project. Next we have the discovery dead zone. You've built an incredible translation agent, and somewhere else somebody's built an incredible data analysis agent and they should be able to talk to each other and work together, but they both exist in two different parallel universes. Because there isn't a

results.title: Sokosumi AI agents vs ChatGPT: A comparison | Masumi posted on ...
results.content:
My work will be boring.
  At least, that’s where it’s headed.
  I’m seeing more and more apps and websites start to blend together. Pretty enough on the outside, but shallow once you actually use them. And I think I know why—teams are cutting corners. Instead of putting in the time for research, strategy, and problem-solving, they’re throwing everything at AI.
  And look, I get it. I use AI myself. It’s amazing for speed—generating mockups, drafting code, even helping me think through edge cases. But there are things it can’t touch, no matter how “smart” it gets:
  -Taste in UI and visual design
  -Understanding real UX patterns (and when to break them)
  -The messy trade-offs in software development
  -Insights from actual users [...] -Insights from actual users
  I keep thinking: in a couple of years, the same companies cutting designers and developers will wake up to cracks in their products. That’s when the hiring spree will start again—because the shortcuts they’re taking right now will come back to bite them.
  Don’t get me wrong, I’m excited about AI. I want to see how far it goes. But just like you can tell when a blog post was churned out by ChatGPT, you’ll be able to tell when a product was designed and built without real design or engineering thinking. And once users notice, they’ll lose interest—fast.
  That’s when UI design and solid software development will matter more than ever.
  So when I see another AI startup telling me my craft is obsolete, I don’t get defensive. [...] ➡️ We hired a front-end js guru
  AI code writers are weak at polishing off front-end UX because they can't "see" the pixels and often lack taste. But, pixel pushing is highly laborious. Adding a dedicated person to handle that final 20% let's us move fast on high value functionality
  None of the above is possible without trust.
  I'm now using SideCoach more than ChatGPT and Asana.
